reviewNotes: Constructing mathematical arguments, logical deduction, precise symbolic statements and connecting language; diagrams/graph aspects are not separately represented in the canonical nodes.
Understanding and using mathematical language and syntax maps directly to precise use of symbols.
Set-theory language plus application to inequalities; mapped to set-language and set-notation-for-solution-sets nodes.
Comprehending and critiquing proofs, justifications and methods maps to critiquing arguments and justifying methods/formulae.
Recognising underlying mathematical structure maps directly to the corresponding node.
Constructing extended arguments for unstructured problems maps directly to the corresponding node.
Interpreting and communicating solutions in context maps to interpreting model outputs.
Evaluating accuracy and limitations through reasoned estimates maps directly to the corresponding node.
The problem-solving cycle is broader than the modelling-cycle node but is the closest match.
Understanding, interpreting and constructing diagrams maps directly to using diagrams.
Translating a context into a model with simplifying assumptions maps to abstraction/simplification and modelling assumptions.
Using a model with suitable inputs maps directly to using model inputs.
Interpreting model outputs in context maps directly to interpreting model outputs.
Refining and evaluating a model's appropriateness is captured at the broader modelling-cycle level.
Understanding and using modelling assumptions maps directly to the corresponding node.
Large data set familiarity is required for statistics assessment in Paper 2.
Official point 1.1 covers proof structure and the three named methods; worked examples are illustrative only.
Includes laws for all rational exponents and equivalence of a^(m/n) with the nth root of a^m.
Surd simplification and rationalising denominators.
Single official point covering quadratic graphs, discriminant, completing the square and solution methods including quadratics in a function.
One linear and one quadratic equation solved by elimination or substitution.
Includes linear and quadratic inequalities, graphical interpretation, set notation and graphical representation of two-variable inequalities.
Covers polynomial expansion, collecting terms, factorisation, division by linear expressions and the factor theorem.
Includes sketching polynomials, reciprocal curves, asymptotes, using intersections to solve equations and direct proportion.
The four simple transformations are interpreted as translations and stretches.
Covers all listed line forms, parallel/perpendicular conditions and straight-line modelling contexts.
Circle geometry including both equation forms, completing the square and the three stated geometrical properties.
Positive integer n only; includes factorial and combination notation and link to binomial probabilities.
Definitions for all arguments and unit-circle introduction are not separately noded; sine/cosine rules, area formula and ambiguous case are mapped.
Graphs, symmetries and periodicity of the three trigonometric functions.
The two core Pythagorean identities.
Solving trigonometric equations in a given interval, including quadratic-type and multiple-angle equations, answers in degrees.
Graphs and properties of a^x and e^x.
Gradient property of e^(kx) mapped to differentiation of exponentials; suitability of exponential models mapped to exponential modelling.
Inverse relationships of log_a x and a^x, ln x and e^x, plus solving simple exponential/logarithmic equations.
Laws of logarithms including log_a a = 1 and negative/fractional multipliers.
Solving a^x = b; the change-of-base technique is not separately represented in the node catalog.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No supplied canonical node covers estimation of model parameters using logarithmic graphs (log-log or log-linear plots).
reviewNotes: Official point 6.6 has no matching node in the supplied catalog.
Exponential growth/decay modelling includes consideration of limitations and refinements of models.
Includes derivative-as-gradient, rate of change, limit definition, sketching the gradient function, second derivatives and first-principles differentiation; some aspects lack dedicated nodes.
Differentiation of x^n for rational n and constant multiples, sums and differences.
Applications of differentiation to gradients, tangents, normals, stationary points, maxima/minima and increasing/decreasing intervals.
Fundamental theorem, integration as reverse differentiation and constant of integration.
Integration of x^n (n not -1), sums/differences/constant multiples and finding a curve from its derivative and a point.
Definite integrals and area under/between curves and lines.
Two-dimensional vectors using column vectors and i, j unit vectors.
Magnitude, direction, component form and unit vectors.
Vector addition (diagrammatic and algebraic), scalar multiplication, triangle/parallelogram laws and parallel vectors.
Position vectors and distance between points represented by position vectors.
Problem solving in pure mathematics and in context, including forces; force-context applications have no dedicated pure-vector node.
Population and sample concepts, informal inference and census versus sample advantages/disadvantages.
Simple random, stratified, systematic, quota and opportunity/convenience sampling; only random sampling and bias have dedicated nodes.
Selecting and critiquing sampling techniques; awareness that different samples can yield different conclusions.
Histograms, box plots, cumulative frequency diagrams and link to probability distributions; frequency polygons have no dedicated node.
Interpret scatter diagrams and regression lines; regression calculations are excluded. Distinct-population sections are not separately represented.
Informal correlation vocabulary mapped to correlation nodes; strength descriptors such as strong/weak are not separately represented.
The correlation-versus-causation distinction has no dedicated node; mapped to the parent correlation node only.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.24, 2.3 (central tendency, variation, percentiles and coding)

evidence.title: Pearson Edexcel AS Mathematics 8MA0 specification
evidence.locator: p.24, 2.3 (central tendency, variation, percentiles and coding)

reviewNotes: Broad summary-statistics point including discrete/continuous/grouped data, coding and interpercentile ranges; some sub-aspects share nodes.
Calculation of standard deviation using Sxx and equivalent summary-statistic formulae.
Outlier recognition linked to box plots and to the quartile/IQR or standard-deviation rules used to identify them.
Critical selection of presentation techniques is mapped to the general statistical-diagrams node; no dedicated critical-evaluation node exists.
Data cleaning (missing data, errors, outliers) has no dedicated node; mapped to the data-classification parent.
Mutually exclusive and independent events with Venn/tree diagrams; set notation and formal conditional probability are excluded.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.25, 3.1 (link to discrete and continuous distributions)

evidence.title: Pearson Edexcel AS Mathematics 8MA0 specification
evidence.locator: p.25, 3.1 (link to discrete and continuous distributions)

reviewNotes: Informal link to continuous distributions and area-under-curve as probability; formal pdf knowledge is excluded.
Binomial model and probability calculations are mapped; the discrete uniform distribution has no dedicated node and mean/variance calculations are excluded.
Hypothesis-testing terminology mapped to hypothesis-test nodes; acceptance-region and p-value language are not separated.
Conducting a binomial test for a proportion and interpreting the significance level as the probability of incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis.
Fundamental and derived SI units plus unit conversion; no dedicated mechanics-units node exists.
Definitions of position, displacement, distance, velocity, speed and acceleration.
Displacement-time and velocity-time graphs with gradient and area interpretations.
Use and derivation of constant-acceleration (suvat) formulae.
Differentiation and integration relationships between displacement, velocity and acceleration.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.27, 8.1 (force concept and Newton's first law)

evidence.title: Pearson Edexcel AS Mathematics 8MA0 specification
evidence.locator: p.27, 8.1 (force concept and Newton's first law)

reviewNotes: Newton's first law has no separate node; mapped to the Newton's-laws parent plus normal reaction and tension.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.27, 8.2 (Newton's second law in a straight line)

evidence.title: Pearson Edexcel AS Mathematics 8MA0 specification
evidence.locator: p.27, 8.2 (Newton's second law in a straight line)

reviewNotes: Scalar and simple 2-D vector forms of Newton's second law; resolving forces is excluded.
Motion under gravity with default or specified values of g.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.27, 8.4 (Newton's third law, equilibrium, pulleys and connected particles)

evidence.title: Pearson Edexcel AS Mathematics 8MA0 specification
evidence.locator: p.27, 8.4 (Newton's third law, equilibrium, pulleys and connected particles)

reviewNotes: Equilibrium, Newton's third law, smooth pulleys and connected particles; resolving is excluded in equilibrium cases.
Formula for roots of ax² + bx + c = 0 given in the AS formula list.
Covers the three index laws listed for AS.
Logarithm equivalence and product, quotient and power laws.
Point-gradient form and perpendicular gradient condition for straight lines.
Sine rule, cosine rule, area formula and Pythagorean identity listed for AS.
Circle, Pythagoras, trapezium and prism formulae in the AS list.
Differentiation results for x^n, e^kx and sums listed for AS.
Integration results for x^n, sums and area under a curve; no dedicated node for linearity of integration.
3D vector magnitude formula in the AS list.
Mean formula for raw and frequency data.
Weight, Newton’s second law and friction inequality.
Variable acceleration kinematics using derivatives and integrals.
Set and interval notation listed in items 1.1–1.23; several symbols have no matching canonical node.
Miscellaneous symbols including proportion, sequence/series notation in items 2.1–2.21; many logical/comparison symbols lack nodes.
Operation symbols in items 3.1–3.10; only square root and binomial coefficient map cleanly.
Function, derivative and integral notation in items 4.1–4.12; limit and increment symbols have no nodes.
Exponential and logarithmic notation in items 5.1–5.4.
Trigonometric function, inverse function and angular measure notation in items 6.1–6.4.
Vector notation items 7.1–7.11; displacement, velocity and acceleration vector symbols lack dedicated nodes.
Probability and statistics notation in items 8.1–8.31; some symbols such as population/sample variance and standard normal pdf/cdf lack nodes.
Mechanics notation items 9.1–9.15; SI unit symbols and time/gravity symbols have no dedicated nodes.
